frameworks: Echo v5 (Go HTTP framework, legacy v1 API)
Huma v2 (OpenAPI-driven API framework, v2 API, bridged via pkg/modules/humabridge)
Traefik Yaegi (embedded Go interpreter, plugin system)
profile.domain_summary: Vikunja is an open-source, self-hostable to-do-list / task & project management web app (AGPL-3.0-or-later, with an added open-core commercial layer for Pro features) offering projects, tasks, Kanban/Gantt/table views, labels, teams/sharing, notifications, CalDAV sync, RSS/ICS feeds, and its own OAuth2 authorization server. This specific checkout is `docsales/vikunja` (git remote origin: git@github.com:docsales/vikunja.git), a company-maintained fork of upstream `go-vikunja/vikunja` — see FORK.md — deployed at tasks.docsales.com via Railway (Postgres-compatible ParadeDB + Redis, per this session's own prior task history) and used internally by DocSales as its team's task tracker, populated by migrating roughly 316 open tasks from ClickUp, with DocSales pink/navy branding applied to the login screen. Based on what's in this repo, it is an internal/employee-facing tool rather than a customer-facing product, and notably its users include AI coding agents that read and write tasks through the bundled `veans` CLI (a bot identity riding on Vikunja's own OAuth2 server).
profile.architecture_summary: Monolithic Go backend (module `code.vikunja.io/api`; repo root /Users/mauriciokigiela/Documents/git/vikunja) exposing a REST/OpenAPI API — legacy v1 handlers on Echo v5 plus a newer v2 surface built on the Huma OpenAPI framework, bridged via pkg/modules/humabridge — backed by xorm over MySQL, Postgres, or SQLite (three interchangeable drivers) with optional Redis for caching/keyvalue/pub-sub. An internal Watermill event bus drives notification, webhook, and audit-log listeners. A separately built Vue 3 + Vite SPA frontend (Pinia, vue-router, vue-i18n, TipTap) is embedded into the same Go binary for single-binary/single-container deployment (multi-stage Docker build, final stage `FROM scratch`, non-root UID 1000, image labeled 2.3.0-docsales) — this fork's instance runs on Railway. Vikunja also ships an Electron desktop client (desktop/), and implements its own OAuth2 Authorization Server, CalDAV server, and RSS/ICS feed endpoints as first-class subsystems (not just API consumers of external ones). Pluggable file storage backs attachments/avatars/imports (local disk, S3, or in-memory, selected by config). A Yaegi-based interpreter lets the server load and execute Go-source plugins at runtime. The repo additionally bundles a second, independent Go module (`veans/`, its own go.mod/go.sum/cmd/internal/e2e) — a CLI, unrelated to Vikunja's own codebase, that wires AI coding agents into this same Vikunja instance as bot identities via the OAuth2 server. The working tree is currently on branch `fix/dark-mode-neutral-background` with one modified-but-uncommitted file (`.claude/settings.json`).
risk_relevant_subsystems: Runs its own OAuth 2.0 Authorization Server (pkg/modules/auth/oauth2server/{authorize,client,pkce,token}.go) — issues and validates authorization codes, PKCE, and tokens itself rather than only consuming a third-party AS; used by external integrations and by the bundled `veans` CLI.

Five-plus parallel authentication surfaces with independently-implemented accept/reject rules: local bcrypt password login, LDAP bind (pkg/modules/auth/ldap/ldap.go), OIDC (pkg/modules/auth/openid/openid.go), TOTP/2FA (pkg/user/totp.go), long-lived personal API tokens ('tk_' prefix, pkg/routes/api_tokens.go). CalDAV auth (pkg/routes/caldav/auth.go) and RSS/ICS feed auth (pkg/routes/feeds/auth.go) each re-implement their own logic on top of these — feeds accept only API tokens by design since feed URLs leak into readers/history/caches; CalDAV rejects password auth once TOTP is enabled; both explicitly reject bot users. Consistency across these paths is worth re-checking whenever any one changes.

Outbound webhooks (pkg/models/webhooks.go, pkg/routes/api/{v1,v2}/webhooks.go, webhook_events.go) let project members register arbitrary destination URLs the server itself POSTs event payloads to — a classic SSRF/exfiltration surface — mitigated by a homegrown SSRF guard (pkg/utils/httpclient.go NewSSRFSafeHTTPClient(), built on code.dny.dev/ssrf) that is manually threaded through each outbound call site (webhooks, Unsplash background-image proxy, Microsoft To Do import) rather than enforced at the transport layer globally; a config flag (outgoingrequests.allownonroutableips) can disable it entirely, and any future egress call site that forgets to opt in silently reintroduces SSRF.

A Yaegi-based plugin system (pkg/plugins/{interfaces,registry,manager}.go, pkg/plugins/yaegi/loader.go, pkg/yaegi_symbols/, example at examples/plugins/example/main.go) interprets and executes Go source code inside the server process at runtime — effectively in-process arbitrary code execution for whoever can supply/configure a plugin; who can reach that surface (admin-only vs. broader) is the key question.

Import/migration modules (Trello, Todoist, TickTick, Wekan, Microsoft To Do, generic CSV, 'vikunja-file') parse attacker-influenceable third-party data formats and replay user-supplied OAuth tokens against third-party APIs; the CSV importer already had a CodeQL-flagged unbounded-buffer memory-exhaustion bug fixed in this fork's own history (pkg/modules/migration/csv/csv.go, commit 14fb9aa).

Open-core license/feature-gating (pkg/license/) phones home to console.vikunja.io / check.vikunja.io with instance ID, Vikunja version, DB type, user counts, host OS, and a container flag, and gates the Admin Panel, Time Tracking, and — notably — Audit Logs behind a paid key. pkg/audit/listener.go is a documented no-op ('license is runtime-mutable — checked per event') when license.FeatureAuditLogs isn't enabled, so whether this deployment has ever set a license key determines whether admin/security-relevant actions are actually being logged at all.

Hardened-but-worth-spot-checking-elsewhere patterns: pkg/routes/admin_gate.go RequireInstanceAdmin re-reads is_admin from the DB on every request (so a demoted admin's still-valid JWT loses access immediately) and returns 404 instead of 403 to hide the route's existence, dispatching an audit event on confirmed non-admin access attempts; pkg/db/dump.go validates DB-restore table names against a `^[a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z0-9_]*$` allow-list after a CodeQL sql-injection flag on that path.

Partially-remediated clear-text-credential-logging pattern in the OIDC module: commit 14fb9aa fixed pkg/modules/auth/openid/openid.go:624 (would have logged the full oauth2.Token via %v, including access/refresh tokens, when a successful exchange lacked an id_token) but left two sibling calls at lines 605 and 610 — log.Debugf("Raw token value is %s", rerr.Body) — logging the raw third-party token-endpoint response body on the token-exchange *error* path. Lower sensitivity than the fixed case since Exchange() already failed by that point, but same file, same category, and worth a direct look.

This fork's own commit 14fb9aa is a primary source documenting prior, repo-specific vulnerability classes and fixes: sql-injection (pkg/models/project_team.go — a cross-package db.ILIKE() indirection CodeQL's sink analysis couldn't verify as parameterized, rewritten inline with a bound parameter; sibling calls in teams.go/project_users.go were individually checked and confirmed true parameterized false-positives, so the same ILIKE-indirection pattern may still exist unflagged elsewhere), path-injection (pkg/routes/static.go needed an explicit path-containment check beyond path.Clean for static asset serving), allocation-size (CSV import), clear-text-logging (OIDC, see above), integer-conversion (pkg/modules/avatar/upload/upload.go, int64->int narrowing before range validation) — plus a separate prior commit hardening GitHub Actions workflow permissions to least-privilege after previously running under the broad default GITHUB_TOKEN grant.

User-facing filter/search query language (github.com/ganigeorgiev/fexpr; pkg/models/task_collection_filter.go, saved_filters.go, task_search.go, time_tracking_filter.go) translates user-supplied filter strings (saved filters, task search, time-tracking filters) into xorm query conditions. No raw string-concatenated SQL was found in this pass, but the expression-to-SQL translation layer (field allow-listing, operator mapping) is exactly the kind of code worth tracing end-to-end.

Bundles a second, independent Go module `veans/` (own go.mod/go.sum/cmd/internal/e2e — not part of Vikunja's own codebase) implementing a CLI that authenticates against Vikunja's own OAuth2+PKCE server, creates passwordless 'bot-<repo>' users, mints long-lived personal API tokens scoped to whatever route groups the server exposes (PUT /tokens), and stores them in the OS keychain or falls back to a mode-0600 plaintext ~/.config/veans/credentials.yml, plus a raw 'veans api METHOD PATH' passthrough escape hatch. Per this session's own recent task history it has already been used to provision real accounts on a live Railway-hosted instance — a second, adjacent bot/credential system layered on top of Vikunja's own.

Broad, file-scattered permission model (projects, teams, project_users, link shares, saved filters, webhooks — each with its own *_permissions.go, e.g. pkg/models/webhooks_permissions.go, project_permissions.go, saved_filters_permissions.go) spanning both the legacy v1 Echo API and the newer v2 Huma/OpenAPI API. The seam between the two API generations plus link-share-token/team-inheritance logic is a typical home for authorization (IDOR-style) bugs.

Rich-text/HTML pipeline spans both sides: backend uses microcosm-cc/bluemonday (HTML sanitizer), goldmark, and JohannesKaufmann/html-to-markdown; frontend uses TipTap (rich text editor), DOMPurify, and marked for task descriptions/comments/@mentions. Confirming the sanitization boundary is applied on every render path rather than only some is worth explicit verification.

Electron desktop client (desktop/, Electron 43 per desktop/package.json) with its own OAuth handling (desktop/oauth.js) and preload scripts (desktop/preload.js, desktop/preload-quick-entry.js) — a distinct review surface (contextIsolation, nodeIntegration, CSP, custom-protocol OAuth callback handling) from the web app.

Real-time WebSocket layer (coder/websocket, pkg/websocket/) pushes task/project updates to connected clients — auth/authorization at socket-upgrade time and per-message authorization are worth checking independently of the REST API's own checks.

Check specifically: (1) bcrypt usage — is a reasonable cost factor used, and is the password comparison via bcrypt.CompareHashAndPassword (timing-safe by construction) rather than a manual byte comparison; (2) is there any server-side password complexity/length floor, and is it enforced on both registration and password-change/reset, not just one; (3) TOTP passcode validation in ValidateTOTPPasscode — is the comparison of the computed vs. supplied code constant-time (pquerna/otp's validator should be, but confirm no extra manual comparison was added around it), and can the same valid code be replayed twice within its validity window (missing single-use tracking); (4) HandleFailedTOTPAuth — does it actually implement a lockout/backoff after repeated failures, or does it only log; trace where it's called from and whether every TOTP-verification code path (both login-time verification and the settings/enroll flow) invokes it; (5) is TOTP enrollment (EnrollTOTP/EnableTOTP) protected against CSRF or against enabling 2FA on an account other than the caller's own; (6) password-reset token generation — entropy/length of the token, expiry enforcement, whether the token is single-use, and whether the reset endpoint leaks (via timing or response differences) whether a given email/username exists.

Return your findings as a list. For each one, give: a short title; the file, as a path relative to /Users/mauriciokigiela/Documents/git/vikunja; a best-effort line number (or range) that you personally read; a concrete description of the issue and how it could realistically be exploited or what could go wrong; and an initial impact guess (High/Medium/Low) and probability/likelihood guess (High/Medium/Low) that it's real and reachable.
